title: Support anchor syntax (.) for first/last child and immediate sibling matching
description:
Tree-sitter uses `.` as an anchor: before the first child pattern means the match must be the first child of its parent; after the last child pattern means it must be the last child; between two patterns means they must be immediate siblings with no nodes between them.

What needs to be done:
- Add an `Anchor` marker type to the query AST (or as a boolean flag on FieldPattern / positional child patterns)
- Update `QueryParser` to parse `.` in the appropriate positions
- Update `Matcher` to enforce anchor constraints using sibling index information from `QueryableTree.children`
- Add parser and matcher tests
- Update `README.md` syntax table

title: Support alternation [ ... ] in query patterns
description:
Tree-sitter allows listing alternative patterns inside `[ ... ]`, e.g. `[(identifier) (string_literal)]`. The matcher succeeds if any alternative matches.

What needs to be done:
- Add `AlternationPattern(alternatives: List[Pattern], capture: Option[CaptureName])` to the query AST in `Query.scala`
- Update `QueryParser` to parse `[ pattern+ ] (@name)?`
- Update `Matcher` to try each alternative in order and return the first match
- Add parser tests in `QueryParserSpec` and matcher tests in `MatcherSpec`
- Update `README.md` syntax table

title: Support quantifiers (?, *, +) and multi-node captures
description:
Tree-sitter query nodes can be annotated with quantifiers: `?` (zero or one), `*` (zero or more), `+` (one or more). This also requires `Match` to hold multi-capture lists rather than a single node per capture name.

What needs to be done:
- Add a `Quantifier` enum (`One | ZeroOrOne | ZeroOrMore | OneOrMore`) to the query AST
- Annotate `NodePattern`, `WildcardPattern`, and `FieldPattern` with an optional quantifier
- Update `QueryParser` to parse trailing `?`, `*`, `+` on patterns
- Change `Match.captures` from `Map[CaptureName, T]` to `Map[CaptureName, Seq[T]]` (or a union type capturing the single-vs-multi distinction)
- Update `Matcher` to bind quantified captures correctly
- Update all existing callers/tests that dereference `captures.get(cap)` as single-node

title: Support multiple top-level patterns in a single query
description:
Tree-sitter allows a query file/string to contain multiple root patterns, each optionally followed by predicates. Krueger's `Query` type holds a single root pattern. All patterns are matched against the tree and their results merged.

What needs to be done:
- Change `Query` from a single `root: Pattern` to `patterns: List[(Pattern, List[Predicate])]` (or equivalent)
- Update `QueryParser` to parse a sequence of `(pattern predicates*)` pairs
- Update `Matcher.matches` to iterate all patterns and union their result sets
- Preserve backwards-compatible single-pattern constructor / apply if feasible
- Add tests covering two-pattern queries whose matches are merged

title: Support unfielded child patterns and ordered child matching
description:
Tree-sitter queries support positional child patterns like `(parent (child1) (child2))` without requiring field names. Krueger currently only allows named field patterns (`field: (Child)`), so queries that match by child position are impossible.

What needs to be done:
- Add an `UnfieldedChildPattern` (or extend `FieldPattern` to make the field name optional) to the query AST in `Query.scala`
- Update the Parsley grammar in `QueryParser.scala` to parse positional children inside a node pattern
- Update `Matcher.scala` to match positional children against `QueryableTree.children` in traversal order
- Add parser tests in `QueryParserSpec` and matcher tests in `MatcherSpec`
- Update `README.md` query syntax table

title: Query DSL: support ordered child patterns
description: Tree-sitter queries can constrain unnamed ordered children with forms like (Parent (Child) (Child)), not only named fields. Krueger currently only parses field subpatterns inside node patterns, so queries cannot express parent/child shape unless QueryableTree exposes a field for the target child. Implement this using red-green-refactor with focused parser and matcher unit tests plus an integration feature scenario. Acceptance criteria: parser represents unfielded child patterns in the query AST; matcher requires child patterns to match ordered children in traversal order; field patterns continue to work; a CST or AST BDD feature demonstrates an ordered child pattern query; existing test suites pass.

title: Lexical compatibility: dotted/hyphenated capture names, escaped string literals
description:
Two lexical gaps prevent real-world tree-sitter query strings from being used with Krueger unchanged:

1. Capture names: tree-sitter allows `@variable.builtin`, `@the-function-name` (dots and hyphens). Krueger's `CaptureName` neotype and the parser only allow identifier-shaped names (`[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9_]*`).

2. String literals in predicates: escaped double-quotes inside string args (e.g. `#eq? @n \foo\`) are not handled — the parser likely treats `\"` as end-of-string.

What needs to be done:
- Relax `CaptureName`'s validation regex to allow `.` and `-` inside the name (while still requiring a letter start)
- Update `QueryParser`'s capture-name grammar rule to match the relaxed pattern
- Handle `\\` and `\"` escape sequences in string literal parsing in `QueryParser`
- Add tests for dotted captures, hyphenated captures, and escaped strings

title: Generalize predicate/directive support: negation variants, #any-*, #is?, directives
description:
Tree-sitter ships a wide predicate and directive surface that Krueger does not yet cover:

Missing predicates: `#not-eq?`, `#any-eq?`, `#any-not-eq?`, `#not-match?`, `#any-match?`, `#any-not-match?`, `#any-of?`, `#is?`, `#is-not?`

Missing directives: `#set!`, `#select-adjacent!`, `#strip!` (and associated metadata plumbing in `Match`)

Also: the parser currently hard-codes only `#eq?` and `#match?`, so custom registered predicates cannot be parsed at all.

What needs to be done:
- Make `QueryParser` accept any `#identifier?` predicate name (passing unknown ones through as generic `CustomPredicate` nodes)
- Register the missing built-in variants in `PredicateRegistry.default`
- Add a directive metadata map to `Match` (or a parallel `Directive` result type)
- Implement `#set!`, `#select-adjacent!`, `#strip!` semantics or at least parse and surface them
- Add tests for each new built-in predicate

title: Support anonymous node patterns, negated fields, ERROR/MISSING nodes, and supertype relations
description:
Several tree-sitter query features require either new AST nodes or extensions to the `QueryableTree` typeclass:

1. **Anonymous node patterns**: string literals as patterns, e.g. `"!="`, match nodes whose text equals the string. Krueger string literals are predicate args only.

2. **Negated fields**: `!field` inside a node pattern asserts the field is absent. Requires a `NegatedFieldPattern(name: FieldName)` AST node and matcher logic.

3. **ERROR / MISSING nodes**: `(ERROR)` and `(MISSING identifier)` are special tree-sitter node types. Unless modeled as ordinary node types in the concrete `QueryableTree` instances this is a no-op, but the spec gap should be documented.

4. **Supertype / subtype relations**: `QueryableTree[T]` has no concept of a node type hierarchy. Tree-sitter query syntax allows matching by supertype. Tracking this requires either a `supertypes(t: T): Set[NodeTypeName]` method or a companion `NodeTypeHierarchy` typeclass.

What needs to be done:
- Add `AnonymousNodePattern(text: String, capture: Option[CaptureName])` to the query AST and parser
- Add `NegatedFieldPattern(name: FieldName)` to the query AST and update parser + matcher
- Document ERROR/MISSING as out-of-scope unless a `QueryableTree` instance surfaces them
- Design and add `supertypes` (or equivalent) to `QueryableTree[T]` and update the matcher to consult it
